retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Etiology -- High-Penetrance Genetic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: Li-Fraumeni syndrome: Mutations in the TP53 tumor suppressor gene significantly increase the risk of multiple cancers in children and young adults, with a 50% risk by age 40 and 80%–90% by age 60. Colonoscopic screening begins in young adults, with a median age of CRC onset being 33 to 38 (refer to the Table in the Evaluation section). [49] [50] Patients with Li-Fraumeni syndrome frequently develop more than one type of cancer, which are listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Etiology -- High-Penetrance Genetic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: While the POLE mutation typically occurs de novo, children of affected individuals have a 50% chance of inheriting it. A study involving 47 POLE mutation carriers from 20 families reported an average of 19 colonic adenomas (range 1–68). Among these carriers, 81.8% had at least 2 adenomas, and 73.9% had 5 or more. CRC was diagnosed in 63.8% of carriers, with the average age at first diagnosis being 40.7. Additionally, 50% of carriers developed duodenal adenomas, and 6.4% were diagnosed with brain tumors at an average age of 30.6. [25] Clinical and colonoscopic findings include: Attenuated adenomatous polyposis (20-100 adenomas). Amsterdam I criteria (for CRC only). CRC and 5 to 20 adenomas, both diagnosed in individuals before age 50. CRC or 5 to 20 adenomas, with a first-degree relative diagnosed with CRC before age 50. CRC or 5 to 20 adenomas, with at least 2 first- or second-degree relatives with CRC, diagnosed at any age. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Etiology -- High-Penetrance Genetic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: POLD1 gene mutation: A study of 22 carriers across 8 families revealed that over 55% of POLD1 mutation carriers had an average of 12.3 adenomas (range 0–45), with at least 2 adenomas in 63.6% and at least 5 adenomas in 55.5%. CRC was diagnosed at an average age of 35.9, whereas endometrial cancer was identified in 57.1% of female carriers at a mean age of 51.4. [27] Clinical and colonoscopic findings include: Attenuated adenomatous polyposis (20–100 adenomas). Amsterdam II criteria (specific for CRC and endometrial cancer only). CRC diagnosed before age 50 or endometrial cancer diagnosed before age 60, with 5 to 20 adenomas detected before age 50. CRC, endometrial cancer, or 5 to 20 adenomas, along with a first-degree relative with CRC diagnosed before age 50 or endometrial cancer diagnosed before age 60. CRC, endometrial cancer, or 5 to 20 adenomas, accompanied by at least 2 first- or second-degree relatives with CRC or endometrial cancer at any age.

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Pathophysiology -- Mutational Mechanisms Associated With Colorectal Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: CIMP is characterized by the methylation of tumor suppressor genes, with tumorigenesis potentially occurring through progressive genetic silencing without genetic mutations. Approximately 30% to 40% of proximal CRCs and 3% to 12% of distal CRCs are classified as CIMP. [76] CIMP is considered a distinct subgroup of CRC, fundamentally differing from other types of colon cancers. CIMP tumors display unique pathological features, including high mutation rates in KRAS or BRAF , a proximal location, wild-type p53, mucinous histology, poor differentiation, and a higher occurrence in female and older patients. [77]

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is one of the most commonly diagnosed malignancies worldwide and a leading cause of cancer-related deaths. Many cases of CRC are associated with lifestyle factors such as obesity, physical inactivity, and diet. Approximately 10% of CRC cases diagnosed are hereditary, primarily due to specific genetic mutations that increase susceptibility to CRC and other malignancies. The remaining 90% of CRC cases are classified as sporadic or familial (without a clear Mendelian inheritance pattern but with familial clustering due to shared genetic and environmental factors). Hereditary CRC syndromes, including familial adenomatous polyposis and Lynch syndrome, often tend to be associated with early age of CRC onset and have a more aggressive course.

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Etiology -- High-Penetrance Genetic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: Gastric adenocarcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Etiology -- High-Penetrance Genetic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: GREM1 gene mutation: This mutation may lead to hereditary mixed polyposis syndrome (HMPS), which is characterized by the development of numerous adenomatous, hyperplastic, hamartomatous, and juvenile polyps at a young age. HMPS increases the lifetime risk of developing CRC to 10% to 25%, nearly double the risk associated with sporadic CRC. This also raises the risk of melanoma, uterine sarcomas, and cancers of the lung, bladder, breast, and melanoma (see Image. Common Sites of Breast Cancer Metastasis). [28]

retrieved_docs.content: Prostate cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Etiology -- High-Penetrance Genetic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: Screening typically begins in childhood, with earlier endoscopic evaluation warranted if symptoms arise (refer to the Table in the Evaluation section). The median age of onset for CRC is 36, whereas gastric cancer has a median onset of 41. [34] Gastric lesions and gastrointestinal cancers occur more frequently with pathogenic variants of the SAMAD4 gene compared to those of the BMPR1A gene. SMAD4 mutations may also be associated with hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, [36] mitral valve disease, and aortic dilatation, which may result in aortic aneurysms or dissection. Gastrointestinal diagnostic features of juvenile polyposis syndrome include one or more of the following: At least 5 juvenile polyps in the colorectum. Juvenile polyps located elsewhere in the gastrointestinal tract. Any number of juvenile polyps accompanied by at least one affected family member.

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer Screening -- Issues of Concern -- Risk factors for CRC
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factor assessments help to categorize the patient as high, average, or low-risk. Aggressive multiple interval-based testing starts as early as the teenage years in patients with a positive family history or co-existing genetic cancer syndromes. A relaxed approach toward screening and further management can be seen in the majority of cases who are at average risk. [9] [10] Family history (especially first-degree relatives) Colorectal carcinoma Pre-cancerous adenomas Genetic cancer syndromes Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) (and its variants Gardner and Turcot syndrome) Lynch syndrome Hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC) Peutz-Jeghers syndrome Juvenile polyposis Medical history

retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is associated with an increased risk of pancreatic cancer, linking this genetic disorder to malignancies of the pancreas. Within the Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Digestive System, pancreatic cancer is grouped with other gastrointestinal tumors such as colon cancer (سرطان القولون) and malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث); these entities occupy child positions under broader parent categories that include pancreatic cancer, malignant lip cancer, and neoplasms of uncertain behavior. Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is explicitly mapped to pancreatic cancer, reinforcing its placement in this digestive‑system hierarchy. Metastatic disease is organized under the concept of secondary malignancies (النقيلة). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic disorder may predispose individuals to malignancies of the pancreas. Within the broader framework of digestive system neoplasms, the CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE DIGESTIVE SYSTEM is organized hierarchically: it includes colon cancer (سرطان القولون) and stomach cancer (سرطان المعدة) as child categories and is itself a subset of neoplasms of unspecified nature or uncertain behavior. Stomach cancer (سرطان المعدة) serves as the parent category for cancer of the stomach, reinforcing the classification of gastric malignancies. Pancreatic malignancies are represented by several interrelated entities. Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is mapped to cancer of the pancreas and is described with the associated morphology of a malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1205
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is mapped to cancer of pancreas, indicating a specific association between this genetic syndrome and pancreatic malignancy. Within the CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE DIGESTIVE SYSTEM, colon cancer (سرطان القولون) is listed as a child entity, while both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and cancer of pancreas are recorded as parent entities, situating these cancers under the broader category of neoplasms of unspecified nature or uncertain behavior. Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland carries the relationship “النقيلة” and is defined by an inverse‑isa link to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان (malignant tumor in the sublingual gland), emphasizing its status as a metastatic malignant lesion of that gland. Similarly, Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant also bears the “النقيلة” relationship, reflecting its ambiguous metastatic potential. The “النقيلة” relationship connects to سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس (metastatic cancer in the head of the pancreas), and cancer of pancreas is classified as an instance of this metastatic pancreatic cancer (isa). Together, these connections illustrate a hierarchical framework in which specific digestive system neoplasms—such as colon cancer, sublingual gland metastases, and pancreatic cancer—are organized under broader uncertain‑behavior neoplasms, linked by metastatic relationships that are crucial for accurate classification, prognosis, and patient management.
